nativeRange.habitat: Subterranean limestone cave pools and streams (cave morph); surface rivers, streams, and ponds (surface morph). Cave populations occupy the Cueva Chica and surrounding cave systems of the Sierra de El Abra region, San Luis Potosí and Tamaulipas, Mexico. Surface populations range from the Rio Grande drainage in Texas south through much of Mexico.

decorPreferences: caves and rocky overhangs mimicking limestone cave systems
subdued or no lighting (cave morph is sensitive to bright light)
smooth-edged rocks and rounded décor to prevent injury from navigation
open swimming areas — fish navigate confidently by lateral line
sparse or no planting (not naturally present in cave habitat)

diet.notes: The Blind Cave Tetra is generally unfussy and accepts most aquarium foods readily, including high-quality flake, micro-pellets, freeze-dried and frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and tubifex. Cave populations have evolved heightened food-seeking behavior and enhanced olfactory sensitivity compared to the surface form, allowing them to locate food extremely efficiently even in total darkness. Feeding 1–2 times daily is recommended; cave morphs may overeat if food is continuously available, as their metabolism is adapted for the feast-or-famine conditions typical of cave environments.

goodWith: larger robust tetras (Buenos Aires tetra, black skirt tetra)
barbs of similar size (tiger barbs, cherry barbs)
avoidWith: long-finned or slow-moving fish (fin nipping risk)
very small fish or nano species that may be harassed

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Grand-Duc; 4500x3000. Sharp group of three depigmented, eyeless cave-morph individuals over algae-covered rock in subdued cave-like lighting — vibrant pink bodies, clearly shows the diagnostic blind/depigmented form and the shoaling behaviour of the species.
Photographer Grand-Duc; 4500x3000. Crisp near-full lateral view of a single cave morph, strong pink coloration and clearly eyeless head — good detailed close view of the diagnostic form.

NIH Image Gallery; 2480x2480, public domain. Sharp side-by-side of the eyed silver surface morph (top) and the eyeless depigmented cave morph (bottom) — illustrates the central same-species/two-morphs story of this entry.

summary: The Blind Cave Tetra (Astyanax mexicanus) is the eyeless, depigmented cave form of a species that also lives on the surface as a normal-sighted fish. It navigates precisely using a highly developed pressure-sensing lateral line, rarely colliding with tank décor. Tolerant of a wide range of water conditions and straightforward to keep, it is well suited to beginners.
careNotes: The Blind Cave Tetra is the depigmented, eyeless cave morph of Astyanax mexicanus — genetically the same species as the surface-dwelling Mexican Tetra. Cave and surface morphs can interbreed and produce fertile offspring, making this one of the most-studied examples of regressive evolution in vertebrates, where traits become reduced or lost over successive generations when they are no longer needed. Eye loss and loss of melanin pigment have occurred independently in multiple cave populations through a combination of neutral mutation and possible linked selection on other traits. Despite the lack of functional eyes, these fish navigate remarkably well using an enlarged lateral line system — a sensory organ that detects pressure waves in the water — and they rarely collide with tank décor. Collisions are most likely immediately after introduction, while the fish maps the new environment; smooth-edged décor is recommended during this settling period. A group of 5 or more is strongly advised, as these are active, social fish and solitary individuals tend to show stress and increased aggression. The cave morph is well-adapted to a wide range of water conditions (pH 6.5–8.0, gH 5–25, temperature 18–25 °C) and shows no particular sensitivity to water hardness, which makes it highly forgiving for beginners. Subdued lighting is preferred to mimic the cave habitat, though the fish generally adapts to normal aquarium lighting given adequate cover. Cave-adapted metabolism means individuals will feed eagerly at any time and can consume large amounts quickly; moderate, regular feeding is preferable over continuous free-feeding to avoid obesity and water quality issues. Very small fish and dwarf shrimp are not well-suited as tankmates, and fin nipping of long-finned species tends to be more pronounced in understocked groups.
breedingNotes: The Blind Cave Tetra is an egg scatterer and breeds relatively readily in aquarium conditions. A dedicated breeding tank of 40–60 L fitted with a spawning mop, fine-leaved plants, or a mesh spawning grid is recommended, as adults will consume their own eggs. Conditioning breeders with live or frozen foods and raising the temperature slightly to 22–24 °C typically encourages spawning. Eggs are scattered in open water and fall to the substrate; spawning can produce up to several hundred adhesive eggs. Adults should be removed immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours at 22 °C, and fry become free-swimming within 3–5 days. They accept infusoria and commercial fry foods initially, progressing quickly to micro-crushed flake. Cave-morph fry hatch without functional eyes — rudimentary eye structures begin to form but degenerate early in development, which reflects the same evolutionary process seen across cave populations of this species. Breeding is widely considered one of the more approachable projects for hobbyists interested in egg-scattering tetras.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Astyanax mexicanus
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided water parameters (temperature 20–25 °C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 5–25 dH), adult size (~9 cm), native range (cave populations in San Luis Potosí/Tamaulipas, surface populations across Mexico and Texas), breeding notes (egg scatterer, easy to breed), temperament (semi-aggressive, fin nipper), diet (omnivore, unfussy), and tank recommendations.

additional.name: FishBase — Astyanax mexicanus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characidae, Characiformes), adult size (max reported 12 cm TL, typical cave morph 8–10 cm), temperature range (15–25 °C), native range (Rio Grande drainage, Mexico), and habitat notes. Confirmed both cave and surface morphs as same species (Astyanax mexicanus, formerly A. fasciatus mexicanus / Anoptichthys jordani).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Blind Cave Tetra Care
additional.notes: Hobbyist-focused care guide. Confirmed beginner-level care, tank size recommendation (~75 L / 20 gal+), group size (5+), water temperature (65–80 °F / 18–27 °C), omnivore diet, fin-nipping tendency with slow/fancy fish, and compatibility notes.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Blind Cave Tetra
additional.notes: Supplemental care data and evolutionary biology context. Confirmed lateral line hypertrophy and echolocation-like spatial mapping, depigmentation, eye degeneration, and that cave and surface forms can interbreed. Noted fin-nipping tendency and preference for groups. Also confirmed easy breeding and egg-scattering reproduction.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Astyanax mexicanus Profile
additional.notes: Additional evolutionary biology context on regressive evolution (multiple independent cave population origins, genetic basis of eye loss), water parameter tolerance, cave adaptation metabolism, and historical trade notes. Confirmed longevity (5–10 years in captivity) and the historical synonym Anoptichthys jordani.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; breeds readily in a dedicated 40–60 L tank with spawning mop or mesh grid. Remove adults immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in 24–36 hours; fry free-swimming within 3–5 days. Cave-morph fry are born with brief eye primordia that quickly degenerate.
